fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A proof is a proof of an argument \Sigma \;/\; B iff, where the proof's smallest argument is \Gamma \;/\; A:
\Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , and
B is A.

Recall that the idea behind proof systems like natural deduction is this: we say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of a particular argument, and then we count an argument as valid as long as there is at least one proof that is a proof of it. It's the reverse of what we did with valuations, where we said what it takes for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument, and then count an argument as invalid when there is at least one valuation that is a counterexample to it.
So what we need is just a story about what it takes for a natural deduction proof to be a proof of an argument. That's where this lecture starts.

From any natural deduction proof, we can determine a single argument \Gamma \;/\; A as follows: let A be the conclusion of the proof, and let \Gamma be the set of formulas that occur in the proof as open assumptions. Remember: sets don't care about multiplicity! So it doesn't matter if something occurs as an open assumption once or twenty times; the only thing that matters is whether it occurs at least once, or not at all. Any proof at all determines an argument in this way; we'll call it the smallest argument of the proof. That is:

Given a proof in a natural deduction system, the smallest argument of that proof is the argument \Gamma \;/\; A determined as follows:
\Gamma is the set of sentences that are open assumptions in the proof, and
A is the proof's conclusion.
Using that, we can say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of an argument:

A proof is a proof of an argument \Sigma \;/\; B iff, where the proof's smallest argument is \Gamma \;/\; A:
\Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , and
B is A.

To put that same idea in different words: a proof counts as a proof of \Sigma \;/\; B when the proof's conclusion is B and the proof's open assumptions are all among the members of \Sigma . It's totally fine if \Sigma has other members as well, but it has to contain all the proof's open assumptions.,

Because of this, every proof is a proof of very many different arguments. Again, this is just like our counterexamples were: a single valuation can be a counterexample to many different arguments at once, and now a single proof can be a proof of many different arguments at once.

The idea behind this way of thinking is this. Think of an argument \Sigma / B as saying that, if we established everything in \Sigma , that would be enough to work with to allow us to establish B. Now, if we have a proof of B whose open assumptions are all in \Sigma , then that shows us a way to do this. If we established everything in \Sigma , then we'd have established all the open assumptions of the proof, since all those assumptions are in \Sigma . And the proof shows us how, if we took those assumptions for granted, we could reason to the conclusion B. So if we've actually established those assumptions, rather than just taking them for granted, the proof would finish the job for us, showing us how to establish B. From this perspective, it's totally fine if there are extra things in \Sigma that aren't open assumptions in the proof; that doesn't get in the way at all.

You can see the smallest argument \Gamma \;/\; A of a proof as being the common core of all the arguments that the proof is a proof of: every argument that the proof is a proof of has a set of premises that includes everything in \Gamma , and has the conclusion A. It's absolutely crucial to remember, though, that any proof is a proof of many arguments, not just its smallest argument.

Recall four properties that a logic might or might not have: reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. We've seen that every logic given by a matrix has all four of these properties. What about logics given by natural deduction systems?

First, think about reflexivity. As long as we allow ourselves to assume any sentence at all, then we will always have a proof of A \;/\; A, no matter what sentence A is: just the assumption of A constitutes such a proof. So reflexivity is assured. If we were to work with a system with restrictions on what could be assumed, then there would be a question about reflexivity. But we haven't looked at any such systems.

Second, think about monotonicity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. Then that proof itself is also a proof of \Sigma \;/\; A for any \Sigma where \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma . This is because of how we've defined what it is to be a proof of an argument. So any logic determined by a proof system in the way we're doing it here will be monotonic.

Third, think about transitivity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A and a proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B. The question is: given that much, must we always have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B? Yes, we must; here's why. Consider the proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B---call this proof P---and think about its open assumptions. We don't know what these are, but we do know that each of them must either be in \Gamma or be A. If they're all in \Gamma , we're all set: the proof P is already also a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, which is what we're after.

But they might not all be in \Gamma . In particular, there can also be open assumptions of A in the proof P, and A might not be in \Gamma . If this is the case, then P is not yet a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. But we can make use of the other proof we've assumed we have: the one of \Gamma \;/\; A.

Imagine plugging a copy of this other proof of \Gamma \;/\; A in at the top of P, wherever there is an open assumption of A. So instead of assuming A in P, we'll instead prove it via the proof of \Gamma \;/\; A we have. If we do this for every open assumption of A in P, we'll end up with a new larger proof of B, and now one where all its open assumptions are in \Gamma . That is, we'll have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B. So the logics determined by our natural deduction proof systems are transitive.

That was notably more complex than reflexivity or monotonicity, so let's look at an example to see how it works. It's important to recall, though, that just one example isn't enough to show that transitivity holds. This needs to work for every possible example. That's why the schematic and general reasoning above was important. This one example is just to give the flavour; but you should look over it while keeping an eye on the general reasoning, to convince yourself this kind of thing is indeed always possible.
This example uses only rules from NM, so it works in any of our three systems. Here are two simple proofs in NM:

The first of these is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; q, and the second of p \rightarrow q, p, r, q \;/\; r \land q. So if the logic this system determines is transitive, there had better be a proof somewhere in the system of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q. And indeed there is: we take the first example proof here, and plug it into the top of the second, replacing each open assumption of q in the second proof with a copy of the first proof. The result looks like this:

See how, in this result, the bottom is made up of the second example proof above, while the top bits are filled in with the first proof. This result is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q; just what we needed.

The key to making sure this is possible is this: all of our rules for building larger proofs out of smaller proofs (which is to say, all of our rules other than assumption, \top I, and LEM) are always applicable based only on the conclusions of the smaller proofs they build on. In the above example, the rule \land I doesn't care whether its inputs are assumptions, as they are in the original example, or proofs that do actual work, as they are in the finished product. The rule \land I acts on them just the same either way. So when we plug in full proofs for open assumptions, we can leave everything else alone and be confident that it will all work out: the result will still be a proof.

It's worth pausing here to think through how this would look with rules that do some discharging hanging around in the proofs. I promise it will work! But it is worth thinking through an example or two.

Finally, substitution invariance. This one is more straightforward: applying a substitution to an entire proof always results in another proof. So whenever we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, if we want a proof of some substitution instance of that argument, we can just apply the appropriate substitution to the proof itself, and we're all set. To be sure this works, you need to check that it works for every rule. After all, imagine we had a rule like this:

Think about adding this rule to NJ. Then we reach a proof system that does include a proof of p \lor q \;/\; p. But this proof system still does not include any proof of r \lor s \;/\; r. The reason is that the rule FAKE RULE is not itself substitution invariant: it applies only to the particular sentences that occur in it. The actual rules of NM, NJ, and NK, though, are not like that. Every actual rule in these systems applies to every substitution instance of anything it applies to. So the logics these systems determine are indeed substitution invariant.

Given a proof in a natural deduction system, the smallest argument of that proof is the argument \Gamma \;/\; A determined as follows:
\Gamma is the set of sentences that are open assumptions in the proof, and
A is the proof's conclusion.
